topic: An employee discovers the company is being sold
raw_output:
[story 1]

Eleanor traced the condensation on her lukewarm coffee mug, the ceramic cool against her fingertips. The office hummed with a strange, brittle energy today. Every interaction felt layered, every casual chat a carefully constructed performance. She noticed the new executive assistant, Chloe, hovering near the executive suite more often than necessary, her smile a shade too bright, her attention fixed on the door. Eleanor found herself meticulously re-organizing the shared drive, a task she usually avoided, her movements sharper, more deliberate than usual. A sense of unease settled in her stomach, a tight knot that refused to loosen. When Mr. Henderson mentioned the upcoming restructuring in the weekly meeting, Eleanor’s focus narrowed, her jaw tightening almost imperceptibly. She kept her answers brief, polite, but inwardly, she was cataloging every shift in the room, every uncharacteristic glance toward the exit.

[story 2]

Mark spent his lunch break staring out the window at the neighboring buildings. The skyline looked different today, sharper, more aggressively modern. He kept checking his phone, not for work updates, but for rumors, for whispers he couldn't quite place. He felt a persistent prickling sensation on the back of his neck, like static electricity building up before a storm. During the afternoon strategy session, when the usual confident pronouncements were made, Mark found himself physically unable to engage. His eyes kept flicking toward the conference room door, registering the subtle shift in posture of the senior partners. When Sarah brought up the acquisition rumors, Mark merely nodded, his voice sounding unnaturally flat. "Interesting developments," he murmured, the words tasting dry in his mouth. He needed more concrete evidence, something to anchor this growing internal disquiet.

[story 3]

From his desk, David watched the flow of activity with detached observation. The usual rhythm of the workday seemed slightly off-kilter, like a metronome slightly out of sync. He found himself lingering by the water cooler, observing the interactions between departments with an intensity that felt almost invasive. When the HR manager mentioned streamlining departmental functions, David felt a distinct tightening in his chest, a physical pressure that made him want to stand up and pace. He started reviewing old company memos, cross-referencing dates and personnel changes, his brow furrowed in concentration. He wasn't looking for anything specific, just trying to impose order on the shifting landscape around him. The air in the open-plan office suddenly felt thin, charged with unspoken transition.
